front: Play: Doctor Rank confesses love and helps the protagonist rehearse the Tarantella; Krogstad blackmails over a forgery
Play: Doctor Rank suffers 'tuberculosis of the spine' (euphemism for syphilis); leaves visiting cards marked with a black cross
Play: a symbolic Christmas tree loses decorations as the protagonist's marriage deteriorates; she sneaks in forbidden macaroons
Play: the protagonist waits eight years for a 'wonderful thing'; the author called an alternate ending 'an act of barbaric violence'
Play: Strindberg wrote The Father in response, arguing the protagonist, not her husband, is the true villain
Play: the title character burns a former lover's manuscript; imagines him dying with 'vine-leaves in his hair' but he dies in a brothel
Play: ends with 'Good God! -- people don't do such things'; the title character shoots herself with her father's pistols
Play: the short-sighted Hedvig is told to sacrifice the title animal but shoots herself instead
Play: illusion is the 'stimulating principle'; a photographer's 'Great Invention' is a 'life-lie'
Play: Hilda Wangel convinces the acrophobic architect Halvard Solness to climb a steeple; he falls to his death
Play: Hilda travels from Lysanger to remind the architect of his promise to build her a kingdom; he fears the 'younger generation'
Play: Dr. Stockmann reports contaminated spa water; denounces the 'compact majority'; compares a party to a 'sausage machine'
back: An Enemy of the People (Henrik Ibsen)

front: Play: Oswald has syphilis and mutters 'the sun, the sun' as his mother considers euthanasia; an orphanage burns uninsured
Play: Irene calls posing for the sculpture Resurrection 'a self-murder'; she and Arnold Rubek are buried by an avalanche; ends with 'Pax vobiscum!'
Play: Consul Bernick pushes the unseaworthy Indian Girl to sail; his son stows away on it
Play: the title banker has lived in his attic since imprisonment for embezzlement; his son cries 'I don't want to work! I just want to live!'
Play: a boy follows a Rat-Wife into the sea and drowns; a floating crutch is left behind
Playwright: Shaw wrote 'The Quintessence of [his] -ism' imagining '700 Philistines, 299 Idealists, and only one lone realist'
Playwright: popularized in Britain by William Archer and Edmund Gosse; Arthur Miller compared his plays to a 'spiritual CAT scan'
Playwright: characterized Norwegian literature after the sagas as 'four hundred years of darkness'; coined 'Friluftsliv'
